condition.qualitative_description: Total surface water body area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct SWBs exist in the MWS, indicating shrinking storage infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In the Chhota Nagpur Plateau (AER-12), surface water bodies — tanks, ponds, and small reservoirs — are the primary irrigation source for rabi and zaid seasons. A declining trend in total SWB area combined with low SWB count indicates that storage infrastructure is contracting, either through siltation, encroachment, or neglect. This directly constrains the volume of water available for post-kharif irrigation. Dashora et al. (2019) demonstrated that siltation of check dams in hard-rock terrain reduces storage capacity and infiltration rates over time, with one 5-year-old check dam in Gujarat found completely silted at 40 cm/year accumulation.

condition.qualitative_description: The ratio of rabi-season SWB area to kharif-season SWB area is below 0.4 and declining, indicating that water bodies dry out rapidly after the monsoon and cannot sustain rabi irrigation.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A low and declining rabi-to-kharif SWB area ratio reveals that surface water bodies lose most of their storage between the monsoon and the rabi season. This is a direct indicator of irrigation failure for rabi crops. Rapid drying can result from siltation reducing effective storage depth, poor bund design, or high seepage losses in hard-rock terrain. Dashora et al. (2019) showed that siltation reduces both storage capacity and infiltration recharge, compounding the problem by reducing groundwater recharge that could otherwise sustain wells through the dry season.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA in the MWS, indicating low historical investment in water harvesting infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low MGNREGA investment in SWC and irrigation structures means that natural opportunities for water harvesting — tank desilting, check dams, farm bunds — have not been exploited. In the Chhota Nagpur Plateau, where rainfall is 1200–1600 mm but highly concentrated in the kharif season, capturing and storing monsoon runoff is critical for rabi irrigation. Dashora et al. (2019) found a benefit-to-cost ratio of 4.1 for check dams in hard-rock terrain, demonstrating that such investments are economically attractive but require active maintenance including desilting.

condition.qualitative_description: No named river passes through or adjacent to the MWS, and the nearest agricultural cooperative is more than 15 km away, indicating limited access to both surface diversion potential and institutional credit for irrigation investment.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: The absence of a perennial or seasonal river eliminates the possibility of diversion or lift irrigation from flowing water, making farmers entirely dependent on stored rainwater in tanks and ponds. High distance to cooperatives limits access to institutional credit for pump purchase, pipeline installation, or micro-irrigation adoption. Together these structural barriers amplify the irrigation challenge by reducing both the water source options and the financial capacity to invest in alternatives.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report that open wells or borewells have become unreliable or dry by the rabi season, or that pumping costs have increased due to greater lift depth, making groundwater-based irrigation unaffordable for marginal farmers.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In hard-rock crystalline basement aquifers (AER-12), groundwater storage is confined to shallow weathered zones and fractures, typically 10–30 m deep. When surface water storage infrastructure fails to recharge these aquifers adequately, wells dry out by the rabi season. High lifting costs from deeper wells disproportionately disadvantage marginal and small farmers who cannot afford diesel or electricity for pumping. This variable is not available in the framework data and must be elicited from farmers directly.

overall_reasoning_note: Irrigation infrastructure failure in the Chhota Nagpur Plateau (AER-12) is primarily a surface water storage problem. The region receives 1200–1600 mm of rainfall but almost entirely in the kharif season; rabi and zaid irrigation depend on water stored in tanks, ponds, and check dams. Diagnosis should be confirmed by at least two of: declining SWB area trend (sig_01), low rabi-to-kharif SWB ratio (sig_02), or farmer-reported well failure (sig_05). Low MGNREGA investment (sig_03) and absence of river access combined with poor cooperative proximity (sig_04) are amplifying signals that explain why the infrastructure gap persists. The hard-rock aquifer context means groundwater cannot easily substitute for surface storage failures. Recommended interventions should prioritise tank desilting through MGNREGA, community-managed storage repair, and micro-irrigation to stretch available water further.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought reducing surface water availability independently of infrastructure condition
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare mean_annual_precipitation_mm and drought_severe_return_period against historical baseline. If SWB area decline coincides with drought years (drought_severe_return_period < 3 years), drought is a co-driver rather than infrastructure failure alone. Check whether nrega_swc_count is also low — if investment is adequate but SWBs still dry, drought is the primary driver. If investment is low and SWBs are silted, infrastructure failure dominates.

confounders.confounder: Groundwater depletion (over-extraction) rather than surface storage failure causing rabi water scarcity
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If swb_count and swb_total_area_ha are stable but farmers still report water scarcity, the pathway is more likely groundwater stress than irrigation infrastructure failure. Elicit well depth trends from farmers (sig_05): rapidly deepening wells with stable SWBs point to groundwater stress. Irrigation infrastructure failure is more likely when SWB area is declining or rabi-to-kharif SWB ratio is low.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have the open wells or borewells in your village become deeper or started drying out earlier in the year compared to 5–10 years ago? By roughly how many feet or metres have they deepened, and in which season do they typically run dry?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If wells are drying by October–November (early rabi season) and depth has increased significantly, this confirms that surface water storage failure is also reducing groundwater recharge, compounding the irrigation challenge. If wells remain reliable but surface tanks are dry, the diagnosis focuses on surface storage infrastructure. If wells are deepening rapidly even when tanks are full, the groundwater stress pathway should be considered as a co-diagnosis.

choices.label: Well depth has stayed roughly the same (within ~1 m)
Wells have deepened by more than 2–3 m in the last 5–10 years
Springs or shallow wells have dried up permanently or for much longer

sources.title: Hydrologic and cost benefit analysis at local scale of streambed recharge structures in Rajasthan (India) and their value for securing irrigation water supplies
sources.relevance_note: Demonstrates that siltation of check dams in hard-rock terrain reduces storage capacity and infiltration rates over time, that manual desilting increases recharge by 84%, and that benefit-cost ratios of 4.1 make such structures economically attractive for securing irrigation water supplies — directly supporting tank desilting as a recommended intervention and siltation as a mechanism of irrigation infrastructure failure.

sources.title: Why is the Arkavathy River drying? A multiple-hypothesis approach in a data-scarce region
sources.relevance_note: Illustrates how groundwater pumping and channel fragmentation interact with surface water availability in hard-rock terrain in India, supporting the multiple-pathway diagnostic approach and the importance of distinguishing irrigation infrastructure failure from groundwater depletion.

sources.title: Climate Change 2014 - Synthesis Report
sources.relevance_note: Provides global context for increasing water scarcity under climate change, supporting the framing that irrigation infrastructure must be assessed against both current and projected future water availability stress.
